Memory and Different Outcomes in Cell Choices Cells that start the same way and live together can look very different. It's not always clear why they look different - it could be random, outside influences, or the cells trying to make their own choices. To understand this better, we watched thousands of these cells for many generations without changing anything. We looked at how they chose between moving around alone and sticking together in chains. We found that when they move alone, there's no way to predict how long they'll do it - it just happens randomly. But when they stick together, they stay together for a set amount of time, which helps them work together. The system that decides whether they move or stick together is made up of three parts that work separately. When something makes them want to stick together, it can lead to forming a group called a biofilm. This means the cells can try out sticking together on their own before deciding if they really want to do it based on outside influences.

The study looked at whether breathing in tiny air particles from the past might make women more anxious today. We studied over 71,000 nurses across the US who filled out a special form about their anxiety levels in 2004. These nurses were mostly middle-aged to older women, with about 15% saying they felt very anxious. We measured how much tiny air particles they breathed in by looking at their homes' distance from busy roads and how much pollution was around them in the month, three months, six months, a year, and 15 years before they took the anxiety test. We found that the more tiny air particles people breathed in, especially in the last month, the more likely they were to feel very anxious. The risk seemed to go up by about 1 in 10 for every extra 10 tiny air particles they breathed in on average over a month. The study suggests that breathing in tiny air particles from the past might be linked to feeling more anxious now.

Cold exposure makes heart problems worse and can make them more dangerous. When it's cold, even without eating more, fat in the body starts to break down faster. This causes smaller pieces of bad cholesterol to appear in the blood. In mice that don't have certain proteins needed for healthy hearts, being cold for a long time leads to more buildup of fatty deposits in their arteries. These deposits grow bigger and become unstable, which is risky. Removing a special protein called UCP1, found in a type of fat called brown fat, stops these harmful effects in mice without the heart-healthy proteins. Being cold also lowers a protein called adiponectin, and giving more of this protein to mice helps prevent the buildup of fatty deposits. These results help explain why cold weather might be bad for people's hearts.

Neutrophil extracellular traps, which are like nets made by white blood cells, contain damaged parts of mitochondria. These traps can cause the body to attack itself and lead to symptoms similar to lupus. We found that these traps need special chemicals called mitochondrial reactive oxygen species to form properly. The damaged parts of mitochondria released outside the cell can make the immune system more active. Injecting these damaged parts into mice causes the immune system to produce a chemical called interferon, which can trigger an autoimmune response. Understanding how organisms change to better survive in certain conditions is a big question in science. It's hard to figure out exactly what changes happen inside them because many parts can be affected, and each part might not change much. We created a way to look at both experiments and computer models together to solve this problem. We used this to learn about how E. coli bacteria become more resistant to alcohol. We tested small changes in their genes one at a time while they were exposed to alcohol. Then, using computers, we looked at how these changes fit together to form bigger processes in the cell, like adjusting to changes in water levels or making the cell wall stronger. Surprisingly, we found that a key way they adapt is by changing how they process and use alcohol inside their cells. By studying the changes in the bacteria that can survive alcohol better in the lab, we learned about natural ways they can protect themselves. These bacteria mostly followed similar paths to become more alcohol-resistant, just like what we predicted from looking at all possible changes in a simplified way.

A study looked at how genes affect being overweight in grown-ups who were adopted away from their birth parents as babies. We compared these adopted grown-ups with their brothers and sisters who stayed with their birth parents. The adopted grown-ups were grouped based on their weight - thin, normal, overweight, or very overweight. We measured the weight and height of 115 full brothers and sisters of 57 adopted grown-ups and 850 half brothers and sisters of 341 adopted grown-ups. The full brothers and sisters tended to have higher weights if their adopted sibling was heavier. For half brothers and sisters, there was a smaller increase in weight as their adopted sibling got heavier. There were no big differences based on whether the adopted grown-ups or their brothers and sisters were boys or girls. However, full brothers and sisters of adopted grown-ups who were overweight or very overweight had much higher weights. This suggests that genes play a role in how heavy people are, even when they live in the same home, and this can happen without being affected by gender, including both small and large genetic influences on being overweight.

In people with celiac disease, their intestines make lots of antibodies against a protein called transglutaminase 2 (TG2) when they eat gluten. We found that there are many more of these TG2-fighting plasma cells than usual in the small intestine. The antibodies these cells make are very good at sticking to TG2, but they don't change much after being made. Unlike what happens when you get an infection, these cells haven't been making new copies of themselves recently, and they can live for a long time outside the body. In 1990 and 2015, we looked at how many young kids around the world were too small for their age due to not getting enough food. The United Nations had a goal to cut this number in half by 2015. We wanted to see how this problem changed in different parts of the world over time. We used information from about 31 million children under 5 years old who took part in health surveys in 139 countries from 1965 to 2002. By using special math, we found that globally, the number of underweight kids went down from about 26.5% in 1990 to 17.6% in 2015. This means there were fewer kids who weren't getting enough food. In richer countries, the number dropped from 1.6% to 0.9%. In poorer areas, it went down from 30.2% to 19.3%. Overall, there was progress, but more work needs to be done to help all kids grow healthy.

Scientists discovered that a part of these bacteria, called curli, which forms when bacteria stick together, can join with the bacteria's own genetic material, DNA. A study using gene tests found that an LKB1–MARK signaling path controls how the Hippo–YAP pathway works. The Hippo–YAP pathway helps manage stem cell behavior and organ size. To find parts of this pathway, scientists used a special test on human cells. This test showed some new kinases (a type of protein) that control different cell activities. Characterizing two types of tiny chemical blockers that stop a specific group of proteins from making new thread-like structures called actin filaments. These proteins, known as Arp2/3 complex, help cells move in many ways. 2177022: Immune cells called dendritic cells move in certain ways based on chemical signals called chemokines. These signals can make cells move in a straight line or randomly, and can also help cells stick to surfaces. We studied how dendritic cells move in response to different types of chemokines. When the chemokine CCL21 was stuck to a surface, it made dendritic cells move randomly but only on that surface because it helped them stick. When dendritic cells touched CCL21 directly, they cut off the part that sticks to the surface, allowing the chemokine to float freely in the liquid around them. This free-floating chemokine, like another similar signal called CCL19, made cells move towards it but didn't let them stick to surfaces. The combination of sticking and moving towards signals helps dendritic cells move in a controlled way, much like they do in parts of the body where immune responses are coordinated. Planar cell polarity signals help shape the developing nervous system during a critical stage called neurulation. Abnormalities in these signals can cause problems like neural tube defects, which affect about one in every thousand babies born. Previous studies in mice and frogs showed that a protein called Vangl2 and other related proteins might help guide the movement of early brain cells to the center of the embryo. Now, we've found something new about how these signals work in fish embryos. We discovered that these signals help align the early brain cells along the front-to-back direction of the embryo. This alignment is briefly lost when the cells divide but quickly returns as the new cells settle back into place. When the fish version of Vangl2 is missing, the cells lose their alignment, struggle to return to their proper positions, and form extra clusters of cells that can lead to neural tube defects. Interestingly, if we stop the cells from dividing, the overall shape of the neural tube can still be fixed, even though some cells are still misaligned. These findings show that these signals play a key role in linking cell division with the shaping of the nervous system, and suggest a new way that neural tube defects might develop.

Folic acid helps blood vessels work better in people with narrowed heart arteries without much change in a certain chemical. BACKGROUND Having too much of a chemical called homocysteine might make heart arteries narrower, but it's not clear if having less homocysteine actually prevents this. Some studies suggest that taking more folic acid, like 5 mg each day, can improve how well blood vessels work, but it's not clear why. To understand this organization, we looked at parts of the DNA that help turn genes on or off, called enhancers and insulators. Some types of brain cells are more likely to get infected by certain viruses, which can affect how sick someone gets. We found that two kinds of brain cells from different parts of the brain react differently when exposed to several types of viruses. Cells from one area, like the part that helps with balance, are more sensitive to some viruses compared to cells from another area, like the part that helps with thinking. Senescent Cells, Tumor Suppression, and Aging: Good Citizens, Bad Neighbors In some living things, when cells get stressed or damaged, they stop dividing permanently. This process is called cellular senescence and is controlled by special proteins that help prevent cancer. While these stopped cells can stop other cells from becoming cancerous, they can also cause problems later on by changing how they behave. These changes might lead to aging and some diseases that happen as we get older, like certain types of cancer that develop later in life. So, the process of stopping cells from dividing might help us avoid cancer early in life but can limit how long we live as more of these stopped cells build up over time.

Leucine-rich repeat kinase 2 (LRRK2) gene changes are a major reason why some people get Parkinson's disease. LRRK2 helps control different parts of cells, and it's thought to interact with structures called microtubules. Problems with how things move along these microtubules might be linked to Parkinson's, but it wasn't clear if LRRK2 changes directly cause these issues. We found that LRRK2 changes with specific names (R1441C, Y1699C) stick to microtubules that don't have a special chemical tag (deacetylated), and this stops important movements in nerve cells and flies, leading to movement problems. In test tubes, adding more of the special chemical tag (acetylation) to microtubules stops LRRK2 from sticking, and a drug that adds this tag (αTAT1) fixes the movement problems. In living animals, reducing certain enzymes (HDAC6 and Sirt2) that remove this special tag, or giving them the drug (trichostatin A), also fixes the movement problems. This study shows how LRRK2 changes might cause problems in Parkinson's and suggests ways to treat it.

This study looked at how giving a special kind of iodine to people with a swollen thyroid gland that wasn't causing problems could affect their health over time. We chose 69 people who had a growing, multi-part thyroid swelling that was pressing on other parts of their body or making them look different. These people didn't want surgery or couldn't have it due to risks. We gave them the iodine treatment once, twice, or even four times. Before and after each treatment, we checked their thyroid's size using ultrasound and tested how well their thyroid was working. After a year, most people who got just one dose of the iodine had a much smaller thyroid. About half of the shrinkage happened in the first few months. People who got more than one dose or whose thyroid started working differently also saw their thyroid get smaller. Overall, the treatment seemed to make the thyroid much smaller and better controlled.

Epigenetic therapy helps fight ovarian cancer in mice by making the cancer less harmful to the body's defenses. Ovarian cancer is one of the deadliest women's cancers, and we need better treatments. The cancer often makes it hard for the body to fight it. In experiments with mice, we found that using two types of drugs—ones that change how genes work and ones that remove chemical tags from DNA—can make the cancer easier for the body to attack. This happens because these drugs cause the body to produce more signals that help the immune system. As a result, the number of immune cells in the tumor area goes up, especially helper and fighter cells like T and NK cells. The drugs also make the tumor smaller and help the mice live longer. Adding another drug to the mix makes the immune system even stronger against the cancer. These findings suggest that these drugs could be useful in treating ovarian cancer in people too.

Epigenetic Therapy Links Low MYC Levels to Better Cancer Immune Treatment By combining two types of drugs, one that removes chemical tags from DNA and another that changes how genes are turned on or off, scientists hope to improve how the body's immune system fights cancer. In this study, researchers looked at different forms of the second type of drug to find the best way to use it with the first type. They created a new treatment plan using small amounts of these drugs given in a specific order. When tested on lung cancer cells in the lab, this treatment led to more immune cells recognizing and attacking the cancer. It also helped turn on parts of the immune system that make cancer-fighting cells more effective. The treatment reduced the activity of a gene called MYC, which can help cancer hide from the immune system, and increased levels of a substance that attracts immune cells to the tumor. When tested in mice with lung cancer, this treatment helped the immune system fight the cancer better and changed the immune cells to be more active and ready to attack. Important details were found that will help plan a future test in people to see if this treatment can make other cancer-fighting methods work better against lung cancer. To sum up, this study shows a new way to use drugs to help the immune system fight lung cancer more effectively.
